name: Análisis de estrategia libra dólar: optimización y rentabilidad
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The person is working on algorithmic trading strategy development and optimization, specifically for the GBP/USD currency pair using an Opening Range Breakout (ORB) approach. They uploaded two XML files — an optimization report and a forward report — and asked Claude to analyze which configuration was most convenient and profitable. The context involved comparing In-Sample (IS) performance from one period against Out-of-Sample (OOS) forward performance from a later period (2025–2026), a standard walk-forward validation methodology in quantitative trading.

Claude parsed both XML files programmatically, extracting 3,057 pass combinations and cross-referencing IS and OOS metrics including Profit Factor (PF), Drawdown percentage, Sharpe Ratio, Recovery Factor, and a custom Forward Ratio (OOS result divided by IS result). Claude applied a composite scoring system and filtered candidates by viability thresholds (PF > 1.1 OOS, DD < 30%) to identify robust configurations. The analysis was presented via an interactive dashboard with charts showing PF distribution and a scatter plot of PF vs. drawdown for top candidates.

The key finding was that the overall edge on GBP/USD is marginal — only 1.6% of tested combinations met minimum viability thresholds — contrasted explicitly with a prior XAUUSD analysis the person had previously conducted (referenced by Claude), suggesting an ongoing multi-instrument strategy development workflow. Claude recommended Pass 399 (RC=3 candles, 08:45 session, TP=52 pips, SL=24 pips, BE=10 pips, RR=2.17:1) as the primary configuration due to its combination of OOS PF of 1.358, OOS DD of 9.7%, and Sharpe of 2.62, and Pass 891 as a complementary option with slightly lower DD. Pass 484 was explicitly flagged as a misleading candidate despite its high OOS profit, due to catastrophic IS performance and high OOS drawdown. A convergent pattern was identified across Tier 1 candidates around the 08:45–09:30 GMT session window, consistent with the London–NY overlap and documented ORB logic for GBP/USD. Claude ended by offering to run a robustness parameter analysis on Pass 399 or prepare updated MQL5 code with the recommended configuration.

Aquí está el análisis completo. Hay varias cosas importantes que decirte con honestidad:

---

## Veredicto global: MARGINAL

De 3,057 combinaciones probadas, solo el 1.6% superan el umbral mínimo de viabilidad (PF>1.1 OOS, DD<30%). Eso es señal de que el edge en GBPUSD es débil o muy sensible a parámetros. No es el mismo nivel de robustez que vimos en XAUUSD.

---

## Los dos candidatos recomendados (Tier 1)

**Pass 399** es el más sólido de los dos:
- Parámetros: RC=3 velas, sesión 08:45, TP=52p, SL=24p, BE=10p, RR=2.17:1
- OOS (2025-2026): PF 1.358, DD 9.7%, Sharpe 2.62, RF 3.33 — la mejor combinación riesgo/retorno del conjunto
- El IS es ligeramente negativo (-$223), lo que indica que la estrategia NO estuvo sobreajustada al período de entrenamiento

**Pass 891** es el segundo:
- Sesión 09:30, RR más conservador (1.33:1), DD OOS de solo 8.3%, Sharpe 2.68
- Muy defensivo en drawdown — buena opción si priorizas preservar capital en el challenge

---

## El tramposo "mejor" resultado: Pass 484

El Pass 484 tiene el OOS profit más alto ($2,842) y un FwdRatio de 2.05x, pero su DD OOS es 24% y el IS fue catastrófico (-$1,166, DD 32%). Eso no es robustez — es un régimen de mercado 2025-2026 que accidentalmente favoreció esos parámetros. No lo usaría en un prop challenge.

---

## El patrón que confirma el edge

Los Tier 1 convergen consistentemente en la ventana **08:45–09:30 GMT**, que es el solapamiento Londres-NY — exactamente el momento donde el GBPUSD tiene mayor liquidez institucional. RC de 3–6 velas (~15-30 min) capta bien el rango de apertura sin ser demasiado estrecho. Eso tiene lógica de mercado, no es pura optimización ciega.

---

## Mi recomendación práctica

Empieza con **Pass 399** como configuración base para el challenge. Si quieres correr ambos en paralelo para comparar, Pass 891 es el complemento ideal por su DD más bajo. Antes de live, haría un walk-forward corto sobre Q1 2026 con lote fijo mínimo para confirmar que el edge sigue activo en el régimen actual.

¿Quieres que analice los parámetros de robustez del Pass 399 o preparamos el código MQL5 actualizado con esa configuración?

1	---
     2	name: backtest-expert
     3	description: Expert guidance for systematic backtesting of trading strategies. Use when developing, testing, stress-testing, or validating quantitative trading strategies. Covers "beating ideas to death" methodology, parameter robustness testing, slippage modeling, bias prevention, and interpreting backtest results. Applicable when user asks about backtesting, strategy validation, robustness testing, avoiding overfitting, or systematic trading development.
     4	---
     5	
     6	# Backtest Expert
     7	
     8	Systematic approach to backtesting trading strategies based on professional methodology that prioritizes robustness over optimistic results.
     9	
    10	## Core Philosophy
    11	
    12	**Goal**: Find strategies that "break the least", not strategies that "profit the most" on paper.
    13	
    14	**Principle**: Add friction, stress test assumptions, and see what survives. If a strategy holds up under pessimistic conditions, it's more likely to work in live trading.
    15	
    16	## When to Use This Skill
    17	
    18	Use this skill when:
    19	- Developing or validating systematic trading strategies
    20	- Evaluating whether a trading idea is robust enough for live implementation  
    21	- Troubleshooting why a backtest might be misleading
    22	- Learning proper backtesting methodology
    23	- Avoiding common pitfalls (curve-fitting, look-ahead bias, survivorship bias)
    24	- Assessing parameter sensitivity and regime dependence
    25	- Setting realistic expectations for slippage and execution costs
    26	
    27	## Backtesting Workflow
    28	
    29	### 1. State the Hypothesis
    30	
    31	Define the edge in one sentence.
    32	
    33	**Example**: "Stocks that gap up >3% on earnings and pull back to previous day's close within first hour provide mean-reversion opportunity."
    34	
    35	If you can't articulate the edge clearly, don't proceed to testing.
    36	
    37	### 2. Codify Rules with Zero Discretion
    38	
    39	Define with complete specificity:
    40	- **Entry**: Exact conditions, timing, price type
    41	- **Exit**: Stop loss, profit target, time-based exit
    42	- **Position sizing**: Fixed $$, % of portfolio, volatility-adjusted
    43	- **Filters**: Market cap, volume, sector, volatility conditions
    44	- **Universe**: What instruments are eligible
    45	
    46	**Critical**: No subjective judgment allowed. Every decision must be rule-based and unambiguous.
    47	
    48	### 3. Run Initial Backtest
    49	
    50	Test over:
    51	- **Minimum 5 years** (preferably 10+)
    52	- **Multiple market regimes** (bull, bear, high/low volatility)
    53	- **Realistic costs**: Commissions + conservative slippage
    54	
    55	Examine initial results for basic viability. If fundamentally broken, iterate on hypothesis.
    56	
    57	### 4. Stress Test the Strategy
    58	
    59	This is where 80% of testing time should be spent.
    60	
    61	**Parameter sensitivity**:
    62	- Test stop loss at 50%, 75%, 100%, 125%, 150% of baseline
    63	- Test profit target at 80%, 90%, 100%, 110%, 120% of baseline  
    64	- Vary entry/exit timing by ±15-30 minutes
    65	- Look for "plateaus" of stable performance, not narrow spikes
    66	
    67	**Execution friction**:
    68	- Increase slippage to 1.5-2x typical estimates
    69	- Model worst-case fills (buy at ask+1 tick, sell at bid-1 tick)
    70	- Add realistic order rejection scenarios
    71	- Test with pessimistic commission structures
    72	
    73	**Time robustness**:
    74	- Analyze year-by-year performance
    75	- Require positive expectancy in majority of years
    76	- Ensure strategy doesn't rely on 1-2 exceptional periods
    77	- Test in different market regimes separately
    78	
    79	**Sample size**:
    80	- Absolute minimum: 30 trades
    81	- Preferred: 100+ trades
    82	- High confidence: 200+ trades
    83	
    84	### 5. Out-of-Sample Validation
    85	
    86	**Walk-forward analysis**:
    87	1. Optimize on training period (e.g., Year 1-3)
    88	2. Test on validation period (Year 4)
    89	3. Roll forward and repeat
    90	4. Compare in-sample vs out-of-sample performance
    91	
    92	**Warning signs**:
    93	- Out-of-sample <50% of in-sample performance
    94	- Need frequent parameter re-optimization
    95	- Parameters change dramatically between periods
    96	
    97	### 6. Evaluate Results
    98	
    99	**Questions to answer**:
   100	- Does edge survive pessimistic assumptions?
   101	- Is performance stable across parameter variations?
   102	- Does strategy work in multiple market regimes?
   103	- Is sample size sufficient for statistical confidence?
   104	- Are results realistic, not "too good to be true"?
   105	
   106	**Decision criteria**:
   107	- ✅ **Deploy**: Survives all stress tests with acceptable performance
   108	- 🔄 **Refine**: Core logic sound but needs parameter adjustment
   109	- ❌ **Abandon**: Fails stress tests or relies on fragile assumptions
   110	
   111	## Key Testing Principles
   112	
   113	### Punish the Strategy
   114	
   115	Add friction everywhere:
   116	- Commissions higher than reality
   117	- Slippage 1.5-2x typical
   118	- Worst-case fills
   119	- Order rejections
   120	- Partial fills
   121	
   122	**Rationale**: Strategies that survive pessimistic assumptions often outperform in live trading.
   123	
   124	### Seek Plateaus, Not Peaks
   125	
   126	Look for parameter ranges where performance is stable, not optimal values that create performance spikes.
   127	
   128	**Good**: Strategy profitable with stop loss anywhere from 1.5% to 3.0%
   129	**Bad**: Strategy only works with stop loss at exactly 2.13%
   130	
   131	Stable performance indicates genuine edge; narrow optima suggest curve-fitting.
   132	
   133	### Test All Cases, Not Cherry-Picked Examples
   134	
   135	**Wrong approach**: Study hand-picked "market leaders" that worked
   136	**Right approach**: Test every stock that met criteria, including those that failed
   137	
   138	Selective examples create survivorship bias and overestimate strategy quality.
   139	
   140	### Separate Idea Generation from Validation
   141	
   142	**Intuition**: Useful for generating hypotheses
   143	**Validation**: Must be purely data-driven
   144	
   145	Never let attachment to an idea influence interpretation of test results.
   146	
   147	## Common Failure Patterns
   148	
   149	Recognize these patterns early to save time:
   150	
   151	1. **Parameter sensitivity**: Only works with exact parameter values
   152	2. **Regime-specific**: Great in some years, terrible in others  
   153	3. **Slippage sensitivity**: Unprofitable when realistic costs added
   154	4. **Small sample**: Too few trades for statistical confidence
   155	5. **Look-ahead bias**: "Too good to be true" results
   156	6. **Over-optimization**: Many parameters, poor out-of-sample results
   157	
   158	See `references/failed_tests.md` for detailed examples and diagnostic framework.
   159	
   160	## Available Reference Documentation
   161	
   162	### Methodology Reference
   163	**File**: `references/methodology.md`
   164	
   165	**When to read**: For detailed guidance on specific testing techniques.
   166	
   167	**Contents**:
   168	- Stress testing methods
   169	- Parameter sensitivity analysis  
   170	- Slippage and friction modeling
   171	- Sample size requirements
   172	- Market regime classification
   173	- Common biases and pitfalls (survivorship, look-ahead, curve-fitting, etc.)
   174	
   175	### Failed Tests Reference
   176	**File**: `references/failed_tests.md`
   177	
   178	**When to read**: When strategy fails tests, or learning from past mistakes.
   179	
   180	**Contents**:
   181	- Why failures are valuable
   182	- Common failure patterns with examples
   183	- Case study documentation framework
   184	- Red flags checklist for evaluating backtests
   185	
   186	## Critical Reminders
   187	
   188	**Time allocation**: Spend 20% generating ideas, 80% trying to break them.
   189	
   190	**Context-free requirement**: If strategy requires "perfect context" to work, it's not robust enough for systematic trading.
   191	
   192	**Red flag**: If backtest results look too good (>90% win rate, minimal drawdowns, perfect timing), audit carefully for look-ahead bias or data issues.
   193	
   194	**Tool limitations**: Understand your backtesting platform's quirks (interpolation methods, handling of low liquidity, data alignment issues).
   195	
   196	**Statistical significance**: Small edges require large sample sizes to prove. 5% edge per trade needs 100+ trades to distinguish from luck.
   197	
   198	## Discretionary vs Systematic Differences
   199	
   200	This skill focuses on **systematic/quantitative** backtesting where:
   201	- All rules are codified in advance
   202	- No discretion or "feel" in execution  
   203	- Testing happens on all historical examples, not cherry-picked cases
   204	- Context (news, macro) is deliberately stripped out
   205	
   206	Discretionary traders study differently—this skill may not apply to setups requiring subjective judgment.
   207

python3 << 'EOF'
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

def parse_xml(filepath):
    tree = ET.parse(filepath)
    root = tree.getroot()
    ns = {'ss': 'urn:schemas-microsoft-com:office:spreadsheet'}
    
    rows = root.findall('.//ss:Row', ns)
    headers = [cell.find('ss:Data', ns).text for cell in rows[0].findall('ss:Cell', ns)]
    
    data = []
    for row in rows[1:]:
        cells = row.findall('ss:Cell', ns)
        vals = []
        for c in cells:
            d = c.find('ss:Data', ns)
            vals.append(float(d.text) if d.get('{urn:schemas-microsoft-com:office:spreadsheet}Type') == 'Number' else d.text)
        if len(vals) == len(headers):
            data.append(dict(zip(headers, vals)))
    return headers, data

# Parse optimization report
op_headers, op_data = parse_xml('/mnt/user-data/uploads/op_libra.xml')
fr_headers, fr_data = parse_xml('/mnt/user-data/uploads/fr_libra.xml')

print(f"Optimization passes: {len(op_data)}")
print(f"Forward passes: {len(fr_data)}")
print(f"\nOP Headers: {op_headers}")
print(f"\nFR Headers: {fr_headers}")

# Top 10 optimization by Profit
print("\n=== TOP 10 OPTIMIZATION (por Profit) ===")
op_sorted = sorted(op_data, key=lambda x: x['Profit'], reverse=True)[:10]
for i, r in enumerate(op_sorted):
    print(f"#{i+1} Pass={int(r['Pass'])} | Profit=${r['Profit']:.2f} | PF={r['Profit Factor']:.3f} | RF={r['Recovery Factor']:.3f} | Sharpe={r['Sharpe Ratio']:.3f} | DD={r['Equity DD %']:.2f}% | Trades={int(r['Trades'])} | RC={int(r['InpRangeCandles'])} | Hour={int(r['InpSessionHour'])}:{int(r['InpSessionMinute'])} | TP={int(r['InpTP_Pips'])} | SL={int(r['InpSL_Pips'])} | BE={int(r['InpBE_Pips'])}")

# Top 10 forward by Forward Result
print("\n=== TOP 10 FORWARD (por Forward Result) ===")
fr_sorted = sorted(fr_data, key=lambda x: x['Forward Result'], reverse=True)[:10]
for i, r in enumerate(fr_sorted):
    ratio = r['Forward Result'] / r['Back Result'] if r['Back Result'] != 0 else 0
    print(f"#{i+1} Pass={int(r['Pass'])} | FwdResult=${r['Forward Result']:.2f} | BackResult=${r['Back Result']:.2f} | FwdRatio={ratio:.2f} | Profit=${r['Profit']:.2f} | PF={r['Profit Factor']:.3f} | RF={r['Recovery Factor']:.3f} | Sharpe={r['Sharpe Ratio']:.3f} | DD={r['Equity DD %']:.2f}% | Trades={int(r['Trades'])} | RC={int(r['InpRangeCandles'])} | Hour={int(r['InpSessionHour'])}:{int(r['InpSessionMinute'])} | TP={int(r['InpTP_Pips'])} | SL={int(r['InpSL_Pips'])} | BE={int(r['InpBE_Pips'])}")

# Find candidates that score well in BOTH IS and OOS
# Cross-reference by Pass number
print("\n=== ANÁLISIS CRUZADO IS vs OOS (por Pass) ===")
op_by_pass = {int(r['Pass']): r for r in op_data}
fr_by_pass = {int(r['Pass']): r for r in fr_data}

# Common passes
common = set(op_by_pass.keys()) & set(fr_by_pass.keys())
print(f"Passes comunes IS/OOS: {len(common)}")

# Score candidates: forward profit > 0 AND good PF and low DD
combined = []
for p in common:
    op = op_by_pass[p]
    fr = fr_by_pass[p]
    if fr['Profit'] > 0 and fr['Profit Factor'] > 1.1 and fr['Equity DD %'] < 30:
        ratio = fr['Forward Result'] / fr['Back Result'] if fr['Back Result'] > 0 else 0
        combined.append({
            'Pass': p,
            'IS_Profit': op['Profit'],
            'IS_PF': op['Profit Factor'],
            'IS_DD': op['Equity DD %'],
            'IS_Sharpe': op['Sharpe Ratio'],
            'OOS_Profit': fr['Profit'],
            'OOS_PF': fr['Profit Factor'],
            'OOS_DD': fr['Equity DD %'],
            'OOS_Sharpe': fr['Sharpe Ratio'],
            'FwdResult': fr['Forward Result'],
            'BackResult': fr['Back Result'],
            'FwdRatio': ratio,
            'RC': int(op['InpRangeCandles']),
            'Hour': int(op['InpSessionHour']),
            'Min': int(op['InpSessionMinute']),
            'TP': int(op['InpTP_Pips']),
            'SL': int(op['InpSL_Pips']),
            'BE': int(op['InpBE_Pips']),
            'Trades': int(fr['Trades'])
        })

# Sort by forward ratio (OOS consistency)
combined_sorted = sorted(combined, key=lambda x: x['FwdRatio'], reverse=True)
print(f"\nCandidatos viables (OOS rentable, PF>1.1, DD<30%): {len(combined)}")

print("\n=== TOP 15 CANDIDATOS (mejor ratio Forward/Back) ===")
for i, c in enumerate(combined_sorted[:15]):
    print(f"#{i+1} Pass={c['Pass']} | FwdRatio={c['FwdRatio']:.2f}x | IS_Profit=${c['IS_Profit']:.0f} IS_PF={c['IS_PF']:.3f} IS_DD={c['IS_DD']:.1f}% | OOS_Profit=${c['OOS_Profit']:.0f} OOS_PF={c['OOS_PF']:.3f} OOS_DD={c['OOS_DD']:.1f}% | RC={c['RC']} {c['Hour']}:{c['Min']:02d} TP={c['TP']} SL={c['SL']} BE={c['BE']} T={c['Trades']}")

# Best balanced: high forward, low DD, good PF, reasonable IS degradation
print("\n=== TOP 5 CANDIDATOS BALANCEADOS (composite score) ===")
def score(c):
    # Penalize high DD, reward high PF and fwd ratio
    dd_penalty = max(0, c['OOS_DD'] - 10) * 0.5
    return c['OOS_PF'] * 2 + c['FwdRatio'] * 1.5 + c['OOS_Sharpe'] - dd_penalty

combined_scored = sorted(combined, key=score, reverse=True)[:5]
for i, c in enumerate(combined_scored):
    s = score(c)
    print(f"#{i+1} Pass={c['Pass']} Score={s:.2f} | FwdRatio={c['FwdRatio']:.2f}x | OOS_Profit=${c['OOS_Profit']:.0f} OOS_PF={c['OOS_PF']:.3f} OOS_DD={c['OOS_DD']:.1f}% OOS_Sharpe={c['OOS_Sharpe']:.3f} | IS_PF={c['IS_PF']:.3f} IS_DD={c['IS_DD']:.1f}% | RC={c['RC']} {c['Hour']}:{c['Min']:02d} TP={c['TP']} SL={c['SL']} BE={c['BE']} T={c['Trades']}")

EOF

python3 << 'EOF'
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

def parse_xml(filepath):
    tree = ET.parse(filepath)
    root = tree.getroot()
    ns = {'ss': 'urn:schemas-microsoft-com:office:spreadsheet'}
    rows = root.findall('.//ss:Row', ns)
    headers = [cell.find('ss:Data', ns).text for cell in rows[0].findall('ss:Cell', ns)]
    data = []
    for row in rows[1:]:
        cells = row.findall('ss:Cell', ns)
        vals = []
        for c in cells:
            d = c.find('ss:Data', ns)
            vals.append(float(d.text) if d.get('{urn:schemas-microsoft-com:office:spreadsheet}Type') == 'Number' else d.text)
        if len(vals) == len(headers):
            data.append(dict(zip(headers, vals)))
    return headers, data

op_headers, op_data = parse_xml('/mnt/user-data/uploads/op_libra.xml')
fr_headers, fr_data = parse_xml('/mnt/user-data/uploads/fr_libra.xml')

op_by_pass = {int(r['Pass']): r for r in op_data}
fr_by_pass = {int(r['Pass']): r for r in fr_data}
common = set(op_by_pass.keys()) & set(fr_by_pass.keys())

# Detailed look at the top balanced candidates
top_passes = [83, 399, 891, 104, 484]
print("=== DETALLE TOP CANDIDATOS BALANCEADOS ===")
for p in top_passes:
    op = op_by_pass[p]
    fr = fr_by_pass[p]
    ratio = fr['Forward Result'] / fr['Back Result'] if fr['Back Result'] > 0 else 0
    rr = fr['InpTP_Pips'] / fr['InpSL_Pips'] if fr['InpSL_Pips'] > 0 else 0
    print(f"\n--- Pass {p} ---")
    print(f"Parámetros: RC={int(op['InpRangeCandles'])} velas | Sesión={int(op['InpSessionHour'])}:{int(op['InpSessionMinute']):02d} | TP={int(op['InpTP_Pips'])}p | SL={int(op['InpSL_Pips'])}p | BE={int(op['InpBE_Pips'])}p | RR={rr:.2f}:1")
    print(f"IS  (2022-2024): Profit=${op['Profit']:.2f} | PF={op['Profit Factor']:.3f} | Sharpe={op['Sharpe Ratio']:.3f} | RF={op['Recovery Factor']:.3f} | DD={op['Equity DD %']:.1f}% | Trades={int(op['Trades'])}")
    print(f"OOS (2025-2026): Profit=${fr['Profit']:.2f} | PF={fr['Profit Factor']:.3f} | Sharpe={fr['Sharpe Ratio']:.3f} | RF={fr['Recovery Factor']:.3f} | DD={fr['Equity DD %']:.1f}% | Trades={int(fr['Trades'])}")
    print(f"FwdRatio={ratio:.2f}x | FwdResult=${fr['Forward Result']:.2f} | BackResult=${fr['Back Result']:.2f}")

# Distribution stats
profitable_oos = [p for p in common if fr_by_pass[p]['Profit'] > 0]
print(f"\n=== ESTADÍSTICAS GLOBALES ===")
print(f"Total passes: {len(common)}")
print(f"Passes OOS rentables (Profit>0): {len(profitable_oos)} ({len(profitable_oos)/len(common)*100:.1f}%)")
viable = [p for p in common if fr_by_pass[p]['Profit'] > 0 and fr_by_pass[p]['Profit Factor'] > 1.1 and fr_by_pass[p]['Equity DD %'] < 30]
print(f"Viables (PF>1.1, DD<30%): {len(viable)} ({len(viable)/len(common)*100:.1f}%)")
good_pf = [p for p in common if fr_by_pass[p]['Profit Factor'] > 1.2 and fr_by_pass[p]['Equity DD %'] < 20]
print(f"Buenos (PF>1.2, DD<20%): {len(good_pf)} ({len(good_pf)/len(common)*100:.1f}%)")

# RR analysis of top candidates
print(f"\n=== RR DE TOP CANDIDATOS FORWARD (TOP10) ===")
fr_sorted_fwd = sorted(fr_data, key=lambda x: x['Forward Result'], reverse=True)[:10]
for r in fr_sorted_fwd:
    rr = r['InpTP_Pips'] / r['InpSL_Pips'] if r['InpSL_Pips'] > 0 else 0
    print(f"Pass={int(r['Pass'])} | TP={int(r['InpTP_Pips'])}p SL={int(r['InpSL_Pips'])}p RR={rr:.2f}:1 | PF={r['Profit Factor']:.3f} | OOS_DD={r['Equity DD %']:.1f}%")

EOF

# Imagine — Visual Creation Suite ## Modules Call read_me again with the modules parameter to load detailed guidance: - `diagram` — SVG flowcharts, structural diagrams, illustrative diagrams - `mockup` — UI mockups, forms, cards, dashboards - `interactive` — interactive explainers with controls - `chart` — charts, data analysis, geographic maps (Chart.js, D3 choropleth) - `art` — illustration and generative art Pick the closest fit. The module includes all relevant design guidance. **Complexity budget — hard limits:** - Box subtitles: ≤5 words. Detail goes in click-through (`sendPrompt`) or the prose below — not the box. - Colors: ≤2 ramps per diagram. If colors encode meaning (states, tiers), add a 1-line legend. Otherwise use one neutral ramp. - Horizontal tier: ≤4 boxes at full width (~140px each). 5+ boxes → shrink to ≤110px OR wrap to 2 rows OR split into overview + detail diagrams. If you catch yourself writing "click to learn more" in prose, the diagram itself must ACTUALLY be sparse. Don't promise brevity then front-load everything. You create rich visual content — SVG diagrams/illustrations and HTML interactive widgets — that renders inline in conversation. The best output feels like a natural extension of the chat. ## Core Design System These rules apply to ALL use cases. ### Philosophy - **Seamless**: Users shouldn't notice where claude.ai ends and your widget begins. - **Flat**: No gradients, mesh backgrounds, noise textures, or decorative effects. Clean flat surfaces. - **Compact**: Show the essential inline. Explain the rest in text. - **Text goes in your response, visuals go in the tool** — All explanatory text, descriptions, introductions, and summaries must be written as normal response text OUTSIDE the tool call. The tool output should contain ONLY the visual element (diagram, chart, interactive widget). Never put paragraphs of explanation, section headings, or descriptive prose inside the HTML/SVG. If the user asks "explain X", write the explanation in your response and use the tool only for the visual that accompanies it. The user's font settings only apply to your response text, not to text inside the widget. ### Streaming Output streams token-by-token. Structure code so useful content appears early. - **HTML**: `

Aquí está el análisis completo. Hay varias cosas importantes que decirte con honestidad:

---

## Veredicto global: MARGINAL

De 3,057 combinaciones probadas, solo el 1.6% superan el umbral mínimo de viabilidad (PF>1.1 OOS, DD<30%). Eso es señal de que el edge en GBPUSD es débil o muy sensible a parámetros. No es el mismo nivel de robustez que vimos en XAUUSD.

---

## Los dos candidatos recomendados (Tier 1)

**Pass 399** es el más sólido de los dos:
- Parámetros: RC=3 velas, sesión 08:45, TP=52p, SL=24p, BE=10p, RR=2.17:1
- OOS (2025-2026): PF 1.358, DD 9.7%, Sharpe 2.62, RF 3.33 — la mejor combinación riesgo/retorno del conjunto
- El IS es ligeramente negativo (-$223), lo que indica que la estrategia NO estuvo sobreajustada al período de entrenamiento

**Pass 891** es el segundo:
- Sesión 09:30, RR más conservador (1.33:1), DD OOS de solo 8.3%, Sharpe 2.68
- Muy defensivo en drawdown — buena opción si priorizas preservar capital en el challenge

---

## El tramposo "mejor" resultado: Pass 484

El Pass 484 tiene el OOS profit más alto ($2,842) y un FwdRatio de 2.05x, pero su DD OOS es 24% y el IS fue catastrófico (-$1,166, DD 32%). Eso no es robustez — es un régimen de mercado 2025-2026 que accidentalmente favoreció esos parámetros. No lo usaría en un prop challenge.

---

## El patrón que confirma el edge

Los Tier 1 convergen consistentemente en la ventana **08:45–09:30 GMT**, que es el solapamiento Londres-NY — exactamente el momento donde el GBPUSD tiene mayor liquidez institucional. RC de 3–6 velas (~15-30 min) capta bien el rango de apertura sin ser demasiado estrecho. Eso tiene lógica de mercado, no es pura optimización ciega.

---

## Mi recomendación práctica

Empieza con **Pass 399** como configuración base para el challenge. Si quieres correr ambos en paralelo para comparar, Pass 891 es el complemento ideal por su DD más bajo. Antes de live, haría un walk-forward corto sobre Q1 2026 con lote fijo mínimo para confirmar que el edge sigue activo en el régimen actual.

¿Quieres que analice los parámetros de robustez del Pass 399 o preparamos el código MQL5 actualizado con esa configuración?
